questions.question: What is a thread?
questions.option_a: A. A complete program that runs independently of any process
questions.option_b: B. A single, independent stream of execution within a process
questions.option_c: C. A hardware component that manages CPU scheduling
questions.option_d: D. A type of system call for I/O operations
questions.explanation: Slide 4 defines a thread as a single, independent stream of execution within a process or application.

questions.question: How does thread creation compare to process creation?
questions.option_a: A. Thread creation is heavier than process creation
questions.option_b: B. Thread creation and process creation require the same resources
questions.option_c: C. Thread creation is lighter than process creation
questions.option_d: D. They are completely unrelated concepts
questions.explanation: Process creation is heavyweight because many resources must be allocated; thread creation is lightweight because threads share resources.

questions.question: According to the lecture, what is the basic unit of CPU utilization?
questions.explanation: Slide 5 explicitly states that a thread is the basic unit of CPU utilization.

questions.question: In a uniprocessor system, how many threads can run at a time?
questions.option_a: A. Multiple threads can run simultaneously
questions.option_b: B. Only one thread at a time
questions.option_c: C. It depends on the operating system
questions.option_d: D. All threads in the ready queue
questions.explanation: Slide 6 explains that in a uniprocessor system, only one thread can be running at any given time.

questions.question: What components does each thread have its own?
questions.option_a: A. Data section and heap section
questions.option_c: C. Text section and file descriptors
questions.option_d: D. Global variables and I/O buffers
questions.explanation: Each thread has its own Program Counter (PC) and stack, as stated on Slide 6.

questions.question: What is true about protection between threads within the same process?
questions.option_a: A. There is strong protection between threads
questions.option_b: B. Threads cannot access each other's stacks
questions.option_c: C. There is no protection between threads
questions.option_d: D. Each thread has its own protected memory space
questions.explanation: Slide 6 explicitly states that there is no protection between threads within a process.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a benefit of threads?
questions.explanation: Slide 9 lists responsiveness, resource sharing, economy, and scalability as benefits; security is not mentioned.

questions.question: What does the 'Economy' benefit of threads refer to?
questions.option_a: A. Threads save money on hardware
questions.option_c: C. Threads are cheaper than process creation and thread switching has lower overhead than context switching
questions.option_d: D. Threads eliminate the need for memory
questions.explanation: Slide 9 defines economy as cheaper than process creation and thread switching having lower overhead than context switching.

questions.question: Which of the following is a primary user-level thread library mentioned in the lecture?
questions.explanation: POSIX Threads (Pthreads) is a standard user-level thread library mentioned in the lecture.

questions.question: Kernel threads are supported by which of the following?
questions.explanation: Slide 10 states that kernel threads are supported by the kernel.

questions.question: Which multithreading model maps many user-level threads to a single kernel thread?
questions.explanation: The Many-to-One model maps many user-level threads to a single kernel thread, as described on Slide 12.

questions.question: What is a disadvantage of the Many-to-One model?
questions.option_a: A. It requires too many kernel threads
questions.option_b: B. One thread blocking causes all threads to block
questions.option_c: C. It cannot run on multicore systems at all
questions.option_d: D. It has high memory overhead
questions.explanation: Slide 12 notes that a disadvantage of the Many-to-One model is that if one thread blocks, all threads in the process block.

questions.question: Which operating systems are examples of the One-to-One model?
questions.option_a: A. Solaris Green Threads and GNU Portable Threads
questions.explanation: Slide 13 lists Windows, Linux, and Solaris 9 and later as examples of the One-to-One model.

questions.question: Which multithreading model allows many user-level threads to be mapped to many kernel threads?
questions.explanation: The Many-to-Many model allows many user-level threads to be mapped to many kernel threads, as per Slide 14.

questions.question: What does a thread library provide to a programmer?
questions.option_b: B. API for creating and managing threads
questions.explanation: Slide 15 defines a thread library as providing an API for creating and managing threads.

questions.question: What are the two primary ways of implementing a thread library?
questions.option_a: A. Kernel space only and user space only
questions.option_b: B. Library entirely in user space and kernel-level library supported by the OS
questions.explanation: Slide 15 lists the two primary ways as library entirely in user space and kernel-level library supported by the OS.

questions.question: What is a thread pool?
questions.option_a: A. A group of threads waiting for termination
questions.option_b: B. A collection of dead threads
questions.option_c: C. A number of threads created in a pool where they await work
questions.option_d: D. A scheduling algorithm for threads
questions.explanation: Slide 16 defines a thread pool as a number of threads created in a pool where they await work.

questions.question: Which of the following is an advantage of using a thread pool?
questions.option_b: B. It allows the number of threads to be bound to the size of the pool
questions.option_c: C. It requires creating new threads for each request
questions.explanation: Slide 16 states that a thread pool allows the number of threads to be bound to the size of the pool, improving resource management.

questions.question: When delivering a signal to a multithreaded process, which of the following is NOT mentioned as an option?
questions.option_a: A. Deliver the signal to the thread to which the signal applies
questions.option_b: B. Deliver the signal to every thread in the process
questions.option_c: C. Deliver the signal to certain threads in the process
questions.explanation: Slide 18 lists four signal delivery options; ignoring all signals is not among them.

questions.question: What is thread cancellation?
questions.option_b: B. Terminating a thread before it has finished
questions.option_d: D. Moving a thread to another process
questions.explanation: Slide 19 defines thread cancellation as terminating a thread before it has finished.

questions.question: In thread cancellation, what is the thread to be canceled called?
questions.explanation: The thread to be canceled is referred to as the target thread.

questions.question: Which cancellation approach terminates the target thread immediately?
questions.explanation: Asynchronous cancellation terminates the target thread immediately, as defined on Slide 19.

questions.question: In deferred cancellation, what does the target thread do?
questions.option_c: C. Periodically checks if it should be cancelled
questions.option_d: D. Notifies the user before terminating
questions.explanation: Deferred cancellation allows the target thread to periodically check if it should be cancelled, per Slide 19.

questions.question: In the POSIX thread example code, what is the purpose of pthread_join()?
questions.option_a: A. To create a new thread
questions.option_c: C. To ensure the main program waits for all threads to finish
questions.explanation: pthread_join() ensures the main program waits for all threads to finish, as explained on Slide 20.

questions.question: In the POSIX thread example, what function is executed by each thread?
questions.explanation: Slide 20 states that the hello() function is executed by each thread.
